expanded_description: Learn to calculate molar solubility from the solubility product constant ($K_{sp}$) by setting up equilibrium expressions, applying stoichiometric relationships, and solving algebraic equations for the unknown solubility ($s$).

core_concept.principle: Molar solubility ($s$) can be calculated from $K_{sp}$ by expressing ion concentrations in terms of $s$ using dissolution stoichiometry, substituting into the $K_{sp}$ expression, and solving the resulting algebraic equation.
core_concept.explanation: Given a $K_{sp}$ value and the balanced dissolution equation, the calculation proceeds by: (1) defining $s$ as the molar solubility (mol/L of salt that dissolves), (2) using stoichiometry to express each ion concentration as a multiple of $s$ (e.g., for MX₂: $[\text{M}^{2+}] = s$, $[\text{X}^-] = 2s$), (3) substituting these expressions into $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{y+}]^m[\text{X}^{z-}]^n$, and (4) solving algebraically for $s$. The mathematical form depends on stoichiometry: $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$ for 1:1 salts, $s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$ for 1:2 salts, etc.

image.alt: Flowchart showing the process of calculating molar solubility from Ksp
image.caption: Four-step process: define s, relate ions to s, substitute into Ksp, solve for s

sections.section_title: Fundamental Approach: Solving for Molar Solubility
sections.introduction: Calculating solubility from $K_{sp}$ is the inverse of calculating $K_{sp}$ from solubility. Instead of starting with experimental data, we begin with the equilibrium constant and work backward to determine how much salt can dissolve. This process requires careful attention to stoichiometry and algebraic manipulation.

key_concept.definition: General Solution for $s$: For $\text{M}_m\text{X}_{n(s)} \rightleftharpoons m\text{M}^{y+}_{(aq)} + n\text{X}^{z-}_{(aq)}$, solve $K_{sp} = (ms)^m(ns)^n = m^m n^n s^{m+n}$ for $s$
key_concept.context: This equation relates the known $K_{sp}$ to the unknown molar solubility $s$; solving requires taking the $(m+n)$-th root

image.alt: General algebraic solution for molar solubility from Ksp
image.caption: The exponent $(m+n)$ determines which root to take when solving for $s$

principles.statement: Molar solubility $s$ represents moles of solid salt that dissolve per liter to form a saturated solution: $s = \frac{\text{mol salt dissolved}}{\text{L solution}}$
principles.application: All ion concentrations are expressed as multiples of $s$ based on dissolution stoichiometry
principles.example: For CaF₂: if $s$ mol/L of CaF₂ dissolves, then $[\text{Ca}^{2+}] = s$ and $[\text{F}^-] = 2s$ because each formula unit produces 1 Ca²⁺ and 2 F⁻
principles.analogy: Like a recipe multiplier—if one cake needs 2 eggs, then $s$ cakes need $2s$ eggs; stoichiometry scales the amounts

image.alt: Diagram showing how s relates to ion concentrations
image.caption: Molar solubility $s$ is the foundation for expressing all ion concentrations

principles.statement: After substitution, solve $K_{sp} = m^m n^n s^{m+n}$ by isolating $s$: $s = \sqrt[m+n]{\frac{K_{sp}}{m^m n^n}}$
principles.application: The sum of stoichiometric coefficients $(m+n)$ determines whether to take a square root, cube root, etc.
principles.example: For AgCl (1:1): $K_{sp} = s^2 \Rightarrow s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$; for CaF₂ (1:2): $K_{sp} = 4s^3 \Rightarrow s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$
principles.analogy: Like undoing exponentiation—if $x^3 = 8$, then $x = \sqrt[3]{8} = 2$; we reverse the power to find $s$

components.definition: The calculation of molar solubility from $K_{sp}$ combines equilibrium expressions, stoichiometric relationships, and algebraic solution techniques
components.interpretation: This formula shows that solubility depends not only on $K_{sp}$ but also on stoichiometry; salts with higher total coefficients $(m+n)$ have solubility that is less sensitive to changes in $K_{sp}$

image.alt: General formula for molar solubility from Ksp
image.caption: General relationship showing how stoichiometry affects the solubility calculation

classifications.behavior: Substitute into $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^+][\text{X}^-] = (s)(s) = s^2$; solve by taking square root

image.caption: 1:1 salt: straightforward $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$ calculation

examples.explanation: $K_{sp} = 1.1 \times 10^{-10}$; $s = \sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$; mass solubility = $(1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L})(233.39 \text{ g/mol}) = 2.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L}$

image.caption: Converting molar solubility to mass solubility for practical applications

characteristics: One ion concentration is double the other
classifications.behavior: For MX₂: $K_{sp} = (s)(2s)^2 = 4s^3 \Rightarrow s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$; the factor of 4 significantly reduces calculated solubility

image.caption: 1:2 salt: $s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$ requires careful cube root calculation

examples.explanation: $K_{sp} = 1.1 \times 10^{-12}$; $s = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-12}}{4}} = 6.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$; note: despite smaller $K_{sp}$ than AgCl, Ag₂CrO₄ has higher molar solubility due to different stoichiometry

image.caption: 2:1 salt follows same $s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$ relationship

classifications.behavior: For Ca₃(PO₄)₂: $K_{sp} = (3s)^3(2s)^2 = 108s^5 \Rightarrow s = \sqrt[5]{K_{sp}/108}$; the fifth root makes solubility less sensitive to $K_{sp}$ changes

image.caption: Complex salts: $s = \sqrt[5]{K_{sp}/108}$ requires fifth root calculation
Iron(III) hydroxide: $s = \sqrt[4]{K_{sp}/27}$ relationship

worked_examples.title: Calculating Molar Solubility of PbI₂ from $K_{sp}$
worked_examples.problem: The $K_{sp}$ for PbI₂ is $7.9 \times 10^{-9}$ at 25°C. Calculate the molar solubility of PbI₂ and the equilibrium concentrations of Pb²⁺ and I⁻ ions.

step_by_step.explanation: Write the balanced dissolution equation for PbI₂
Define $s$ as molar solubility and express ion concentrations in terms of $s$
Write the $K_{sp}$ expression and substitute ion concentrations
Substitute the known $K_{sp}$ value and solve for $s$
Take the cube root to find $s$

worked_examples.final_answer: Molar solubility of PbI₂ = $1.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$; $[\text{Pb}^{2+}] = 1.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$; $[\text{I}^-] = 2.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using stoichiometry and algebra to calculate molar solubility from $K_{sp}$

image.caption: Step-by-step calculation of molar solubility from $K_{sp}$

worked_examples.title: Comparing Solubilities: AgCl vs Ag₂CrO₄
worked_examples.problem: Given $K_{sp}(\text{AgCl}) = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$ and $K_{sp}(\text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4) = 1.1 \times 10^{-12}$, calculate and compare the molar solubilities of both salts.

step_by_step.explanation: Interpret the result: despite having a smaller $K_{sp}$, Ag₂CrO₄ is more soluble
step_by_step.calculation: Ratio: $\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.3 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 5$; Ag₂CrO₄ is ~5× more soluble than AgCl

worked_examples.final_answer: AgCl: $s = 1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$; Ag₂CrO₄: $s = 6.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$; Ag₂CrO₄ is more soluble despite smaller $K_{sp}$ due to different stoichiometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Stoichiometry affects solubility ranking; $K_{sp}$ values can only be directly compared for salts with identical ion ratios

image.alt: Comparison of AgCl and Ag2CrO4 solubilities
image.caption: Visual comparison showing why stoichiometry matters in solubility rankings

sections.introduction: Following a consistent five-step procedure ensures accurate calculation of molar solubility from $K_{sp}$ values. This method works for any ionic compound regardless of stoichiometric complexity.
sections.key_concept: Five-Step Method: (1) Write balanced dissolution equation, (2) Define $s$ and express ion concentrations, (3) Write $K_{sp}$ expression and substitute, (4) Solve algebraically for $s$, (5) Calculate ion concentrations and verify

process_flow.description: Write the equilibrium equation showing the solid salt dissociating into aqueous ions with correct stoichiometry
process_flow.mechanism: Include phase labels (s) and (aq); balance atoms and charge; verify coefficients match the salt formula

image.caption: Step 1: Start with the correct balanced dissolution equation

process_flow.description: Let $s$ = molar solubility (mol/L of salt dissolved); express each ion concentration as a multiple of $s$ using stoichiometry
process_flow.mechanism: For each mole of salt dissolved: $[\text{M}^{y+}] = ms$ and $[\text{X}^{z-}] = ns$ where $m$ and $n$ are stoichiometric coefficients
process_flow.result: Ion concentrations expressed in terms of $s$: e.g., for CaF₂: $[\text{Ca}^{2+}] = s$, $[\text{F}^-] = 2s$

image.alt: Relating ion concentrations to molar solubility
image.caption: Step 2: Apply stoichiometry to express ions in terms of $s$

process_flow.description: Write the $K_{sp}$ expression and substitute the ion concentration expressions from Step 2
process_flow.mechanism: $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{y+}]^m[\text{X}^{z-}]^n = (ms)^m(ns)^n = m^m n^n s^{m+n}$; simplify to isolate powers of $s$

image.caption: Step 3: Substitute and simplify to get equation in terms of $s$

process_flow.description: Isolate $s$ by dividing by the coefficient factor and taking the $(m+n)$-th root
process_flow.mechanism: $s = \sqrt[m+n]{\frac{K_{sp}}{m^m n^n}}$; use calculator carefully for roots of very small numbers
process_flow.result: Numerical value of molar solubility $s$ in mol/L

image.caption: Step 4: Solve for $s$ using appropriate root

process_flow.description: Use the value of $s$ to find equilibrium ion concentrations; optionally verify by recalculating $K_{sp}$
process_flow.mechanism: Substitute $s$ back into $[\text{M}^{y+}] = ms$ and $[\text{X}^{z-}] = ns$; check that $(ms)^m(ns)^n \approx K_{sp}$
process_flow.result: Complete solution: molar solubility and all equilibrium ion concentrations with verification

image.caption: Step 5: Calculate ion concentrations and verify the result

worked_examples.title: Complete Workflow: Solubility of Mg(OH)₂ from $K_{sp}$
worked_examples.problem: The $K_{sp}$ for Mg(OH)₂ is $1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ at 25°C. Calculate the molar solubility of Mg(OH)₂ and the equilibrium concentration of OH⁻ ions.
worked_examples.final_answer: Molar solubility of Mg(OH)₂ = $1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$; $[\text{OH}^-] = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Five-step systematic procedure for calculating solubility from $K_{sp}$

image.caption: Full application of the five-step method for a 1:2 salt

sections.introduction: In some solubility calculations, approximations simplify the algebra without significantly affecting accuracy. Understanding when approximations are valid and how to handle special cases improves calculation efficiency and reliability.

key_concept.definition: Small-$s$ Approximation: When $s$ is very small relative to initial concentrations (e.g., in common ion problems), terms like $(C + ns) \approx C$ simplify calculations
key_concept.context: This approximation is valid when $ns \ll C$ (typically when $s < 0.05C$); always verify the assumption after solving

image.alt: Diagram showing when small-s approximation is valid
image.caption: The approximation $(C + ns) \approx C$ is valid when the added amount is negligible

categories.description: In pure water, no initial ions are present; all ion concentrations come solely from salt dissolution
categories.detailed_mechanism: For any salt in pure water: initial $[\text{M}^{y+}]_0 = [\text{X}^{z-}]_0 = 0$; equilibrium concentrations depend only on $s$ and stoichiometry

examples.explanation: No initial Ca²⁺ or F⁻; $[\text{Ca}^{2+}] = s$, $[\text{F}^-] = 2s$; $K_{sp} = 4s^3$ solved directly for $s$

image.caption: Pure water: simplest case with no initial ions

categories.description: When a common ion is already present, the solubility calculation includes an initial concentration term
categories.detailed_mechanism: For MX₂ in solution with initial $[\text{X}^-]_0 = C$: $[\text{M}^{2+}] = s$, $[\text{X}^-] = C + 2s$; if $2s \ll C$, approximate $[\text{X}^-] \approx C$ and solve $K_{sp} \approx sC^2$

image.caption: Common ions dramatically reduce solubility; approximation simplifies calculation

categories.description: For salts with basic anions (CO₃²⁻, S²⁻, OH⁻, etc.), solubility increases in acidic solution due to anion protonation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Basic anions react with H⁺: $\text{X}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{HX}$; removing X⁻ shifts dissolution equilibrium right, increasing apparent solubility beyond the $K_{sp}$-predicted value

examples.explanation: $\text{CaCO}_{3(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CO}_3^{2-}$; then $\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_3^-$; low pH removes CO₃²⁻, increasing CaCO₃ solubility

image.caption: Acidic conditions increase solubility of salts with basic anions

worked_examples.title: Verifying the Small-$s$ Approximation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the solubility of AgCl in 0.010 M NaCl ($K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$) using the approximation $[\text{Cl}^-] \approx 0.010 \text{ M}$, then verify the approximation is valid.

step_by_step.explanation: Set up concentrations with initial $[\text{Cl}^-]_0 = 0.010 \text{ M}$

step_by_step.calculation: Exact: $1.8 \times 10^{-10} = s(0.010 + s)$; quadratic solution gives $s = 1.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ M}$ (identical to approximation)

worked_examples.final_answer: Solubility of AgCl in 0.010 M NaCl = $1.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ M}$; approximation is valid because $s$ is only 0.00018% of 0.010 M
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using and verifying the small-$s$ approximation in common ion solubility calculations

image.caption: Always verify approximations by checking that the neglected term is truly negligible

summary: Calculating molar solubility ($s$) from $K_{sp}$ requires a systematic five-step approach: (1) write the balanced dissolution equation, (2) define $s$ and express ion concentrations using stoichiometry ($[\text{M}^{y+}] = ms$, $[\text{X}^{z-}] = ns$), (3) substitute into the $K_{sp}$ expression to get $K_{sp} = m^m n^n s^{m+n}$, (4) solve algebraically for $s = \sqrt[m+n]{K_{sp}/(m^m n^n)}$, and (5) calculate ion concentrations and verify the result. The mathematical form depends on stoichiometry: $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$ for 1:1 salts, $s = \sqrt[3]{K_{sp}/4}$ for 1:2 salts, etc. Direct comparison of $K_{sp}$ values to rank solubilities is valid only for salts with identical stoichiometry. Approximations like $[\text{X}^-] \approx C$ when a common ion is present simplify calculations but must be verified post-solution. Mastery of these calculations enables prediction of precipitation behavior, design of separation schemes, and understanding of solubility trends in chemical and environmental systems.
